kept.text:
$TRMB Q1 2026 earnings: Record ARR and Margin Expansion Validate the 'Connect & Scale' Strategy

Trimble delivered a powerful first quarter in 2026, proving that its software-first transformation is accelerating. Revenue grew 12% YoY to $939.9 million, completing a hard reversal from the declines seen early last year. The real star, however, is Annualized Recurring Revenue (ARR), which hit a record $2.43 billion. This structural shift to higher-quality revenue drove Non-GAAP gross margins to 71.0% and unleashed massive free cash flow ($268.6 million), comfortably funding $317 million in share buybacks. While the AECO segment is booming, the Transportation & Logistics segment remains a stubborn top-line laggard. Overall, the business is compounding returns, and management's bold long-term targets look increasingly achievable.

Full article with charts - link in bio

🐂 𝐁𝐮𝐥𝐥 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐞
• 𝐁𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐌𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐥 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐬 𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 — The Connect & Scale strategy is successfully shifting the company away from cyclical hardware. ARR is up 12% to $2.43 billion, fueling a record Non-GAAP gross margin of 71.0%.
• 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐡 𝐌𝐚𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐞 — Free cash flow surged 80% YoY to $268.6 million in the quarter. Management is aggressively returning this to shareholders, repurchasing nearly 4.7 million shares for $317 million.

🐻 𝐁𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐞
• 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐒𝐞𝐠𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐃𝐫𝐚𝐠 — The Transportation & Logistics (T&L) segment remains mired in a freight recession, with revenue shrinking 4.4% YoY to $139.6 million. It is a persistent drag on the broader top-line recovery.
• 𝐌𝐚𝐫𝐠𝐢𝐧 𝐋𝐞𝐚𝐤𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐅𝐢𝐞𝐥𝐝 𝐒𝐲𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐬 — Despite a 14% jump in Field Systems revenue, the segment's operating margin actually contracted from 29.7% to 28.8%, suggesting rising costs or unfavorable product mix shifts.

⚖️ 𝐕𝐞𝐫𝐝𝐢𝐜𝐭: 🟢
Bullish. The transition to a predictable, recurring-revenue powerhouse is accelerating. A 26% Non-GAAP operating margin and double-digit ARR growth provide a deep competitive moat, eclipsing the isolated weakness in the Transportation segment.

𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬

🟢🟢 𝐀𝐄𝐂𝐎 𝐒𝐞𝐠𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐢𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐲 𝐆𝐫𝐨𝐰𝐭𝐡 𝐄𝐧𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐞
The Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Owner (AECO) segment is firing on all cylinders. Accelerating its momentum from previous quarters, AECO revenue surged 16.6% YoY to $391.1 million. More impressively, the segment demonstrated tremendous operating leverage, expanding its operating margin from 27.3% in 25Q1 to 31.5% in 26Q1. The cross-selling of software bundles under the Trimble Construction One banner is yielding massive dividends.

🟢 𝐑𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐥𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡 𝐓𝐨𝐰𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐒𝐮𝐛𝐬𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐒𝐨𝐟𝐭𝐰𝐚𝐫𝐞
Trimble is successfully rewiring its DNA. Subscription and services revenue reached $628.7 million, making up 67% of total revenue, up from $569.0 million a year ago. This predictable base is structurally lifting the company's gross and operating margins, rendering the business far more resilient to cyclical equipment downturns than in its past.

🟢 𝐀𝐈 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐀𝐧𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐨𝐩𝐢𝐜'𝐬 𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐮𝐝𝐞 [NEW]
Management is operationalizing its "AI-forward" vision. A specialized integration linking SketchUp with Anthropic's Claude language model brings conversational AI capabilities directly into 3D modeling. This translates to the company moving beyond task automation into generative design, utilizing its unique industry data corpus to lock customers into the ecosystem.

🔴 𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 & 𝐋𝐨𝐠𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐜𝐬 𝐒𝐞𝐠𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐠
The T&L segment remains the weakest link in the portfolio. Revenue reversed into a contraction, falling 4.4% YoY to $139.6 million. While management managed to squeeze out higher operating margins (24.2% vs 17.9% YoY) through aggressive cost controls, the top-line deceleration highlights an ongoing struggle against a stubbornly challenged freight macro environment.

⚪ 𝐅𝐢𝐞𝐥𝐝 𝐒𝐲𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐫𝐠𝐢𝐧 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 [NEW]
A clear contradiction to the company's broader "record margin" narrative exists within the Field Systems segment. While the segment's revenue grew a healthy 13.9% YoY, its operating margin dropped from 29.7% to 28.8%. Cost of sales in this segment spiked by $20.8 million, indicating that either product mix, hardware discounting, or tariff absorption is eating into profitability.

🔴 𝐏𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐜𝐫𝐨 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐟𝐟 𝐑𝐢𝐬𝐤𝐬
The company's safe harbor explicitly warns of heightened trade tensions, tariff uncertainties, and associated supply chain disruptions. In prior quarters, management cited a $10 million per quarter tariff hit. With the global macroeconomic outlook remaining unstable, physical product lines (which still generated $311 million in Q1) remain vulnerable to margin shocks.

𝐎𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 𝐊𝐏𝐈𝐬

𝐅𝐫𝐞𝐞 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐡 𝐅𝐥𝐨𝐰 (𝟐𝟔𝐐𝟏): $268.6 million
Accelerating. Free cash flow surged 80% from $149.0 million in the prior year period. This was driven by a $119 million increase in net cash provided by operating activities, largely due to excellent working capital management and higher net income. Capital expenditures remained stable and low at $6.1 million.

𝐒𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐮𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐬𝐞𝐬 (𝟐𝟔𝐐𝟏): $316.9 million
Stable and aggressive capital return. Trimble repurchased roughly 4.7 million shares in the quarter. While down from the massive $627 million repurchased in 25Q1, it indicates management's continued belief that the stock is undervalued and serves as a highly accretive use of the expanding free cash flow base.

𝐍𝐨𝐧-𝐆𝐀𝐀𝐏 𝐆𝐫𝐨𝐬𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐫𝐠𝐢𝐧 (𝟐𝟔𝐐𝟏): 71.0%
Accelerating. Up 180 basis points from 69.2% in 25Q1. This structural improvement is the direct mathematical result of the mix shift toward high-margin subscription and software revenues, absorbing the shocks of hardware input costs.

𝐆𝐮𝐢𝐝𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞

𝟐𝟔𝐐𝟐 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞: $938 - $963 million
Decelerating. The midpoint of $950.5 million implies an 8.5% YoY growth rate, stepping down from the 12% growth achieved in 26Q1. Sequentially, it implies a very modest 1.1% QoQ growth. This suggests management is pricing in a normalization of base effects or applying macro conservatism.

𝟐𝟔𝐐𝟐 𝐍𝐨𝐧-𝐆𝐀𝐀𝐏 𝐄𝐏𝐒: $0.78 - $0.82
Stable. The $0.80 midpoint implies a 12.7% YoY growth rate over the $0.71 delivered in 25Q2. Earnings are guided to grow faster than revenue, proving that the operating leverage from the software transition remains firmly intact.

𝐅𝐘𝟐𝟔 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞: $3,835 - $3,915 million
Stable. The midpoint of $3.875 billion implies an approximate 8.5% YoY growth rate for the full year. This is consistent with management's early signals in late 2025 and keeps the company firmly on the glide path toward its 2027 goal of $4.0 billion in total revenue.

𝐅𝐘𝟐𝟔 𝐍𝐨𝐧-𝐆𝐀𝐀𝐏 𝐄𝐏𝐒: $3.47 - $3.64
Accelerating. The $3.555 midpoint implies roughly 13.5% YoY growth compared to FY25. Management expects the combination of expanding software margins, share count reduction from buybacks, and tight cost controls to continue driving outsized bottom-line expansion.

𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐐𝐮𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬

𝐅𝐢𝐞𝐥𝐝 𝐒𝐲𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐬 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐟𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲
Field Systems revenue grew an impressive 14% this quarter, yet the segment's operating margin compressed. Are model conversions, hardware discounting, or unmitigated tariffs driving this margin leak, and should we expect this to persist through the year?

𝐓&𝐋 𝐓𝐮𝐫𝐧𝐚𝐫𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐝 𝐓𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞
With Transportation & Logistics revenue shrinking 4% year-over-year, is there a timeline to return this segment to top-line growth, or is the strategy here purely focused on extracting higher operating margins while the freight recession plays out?

𝐆𝐮𝐢𝐝𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐞𝐫𝐯𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐬𝐦
The Q2 guidance implies a deceleration in YoY revenue growth from 12% to 8.5%. Is this moderation strictly a function of tougher year-over-year comparables, or are you seeing elongation in enterprise sales cycles or macroeconomic hesitation in the pipeline?

𝐀𝐈 𝐌𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐢𝐳𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐲
With the integration of Anthropic's Claude into SketchUp, what is the specific monetization pathway for these advanced AI features? Will they drive premium tier upgrades, or will they require a shift toward consumption-based pricing models?

kept.text: $TRMB just missed by the most it has in at least 5 quarters. eps came in at $0.42 vs $0.58 estimate - a 27.6% miss. after four straight beats (Q1 +4.4%, Q2 +13.6%, Q3 +13.0%, Q4 +4.1%), the sandbagging playbook just broke. revenue hit $939.9M with no consensus estimate to compare against, but the trajectory tells the story: annual revenue has declined three straight years from $3.80B (2023) to $3.68B (2024) to $3.59B (2025). op margin at 17.0% is already 500bps below the sector median of 22.0%. gross margins are 71.1% - the software transition is real - but ROIC at 6.7% sits 11.3 points below the sector median of 18.0%. the EPS ramp from $0.61 to $1.00 over the prior four quarters was built on cost cuts, not revenue. this quarter proves it.

Traders, copper is not sleeping - it is trading around $6.1306/lb, up +$0.1876 / +3.16% on the day. That is the kind of move that puts the whole copper sector back on radar.

But I’m not only watching price. I’m watching supply chains. Around BC / Western Canada, $NRED / $NREDF has a better regional setup in my view because there are 4 acid supply/manufacturing channels to watch: Teck Trail/IRM, SEASTAR Sidney, Chemtrade BC/Washington, and Sherritt Alberta.

Less supply headache matters when sulfuric acid is becoming a copper bottleneck.

Add Wilmac at 16,077.76 ha, 70% option on 4,573.82 ha, and $8.5M exploration path.

My price target: $15.
Trading is risky. Size accordingly.
